\title{\textbf{Target-Specific Recoverability Maps for Reduced-Lead ECG
Reconstruction}\\[2pt]\large A graded per-lead identifiability + conditioning certificate with
calibrated intervals, and an honest account of an abandoned fabrication claim}
% Double-blind toggle: \anontrue for a double-blind venue submission; \anonfalse for arXiv.
\newif\ifanon\anonfalse
\ifanon\author{Anonymous authors\\ Paper under double-blind review}
\else\author{Weiping Yan\\ University of Minnesota, Twin Cities}\fi
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Reconstructing missing electrocardiogram (ECG) leads from a reduced set is ill-posed, and a
scalar reconstruction error says nothing about \emph{which} named feature's low-rank (dipolar)
component, on \emph{which} lead, is even recoverable. We make this a target-specific, computable
certificate. For a waveform segment $s$ (P/QRS/ST/T), an observed lead set $S$, and a target lead
$\ell$, two closed-form numbers from one truncated SVD of the segment's empirical rank-3 spatial
sub-matrix give a graded \emph{identifiability} $\eta_{s,\ell}(S)$ (its exact value zero iff the
target's dipolar component is recoverable from $S$) and a \emph{conditioning} $\kappa_{s,\ell}(S)$.
To the map we attach an exact per-lead \emph{minimax ambiguity floor} $a_{s,\ell}(S)$---the exact
minimax risk of estimating the target's dipolar functional over a second-moment class, attained by a
Gaussian least-favourable prior---which on real data \emph{predicts} the measured per-lead dipolar
error of the reconstructors we evaluate (Spearman $\CertValSpearAmb$, AUC $\CertValAUC$); it is a
worst-case-prior floor, so estimators exploiting predictable non-Gaussian structure fall below it,
and that gap is exactly the residual our distribution-free calibrated intervals cover. On PTB-XL,
precordial ST is \emph{exactly} non-identifiable from limb leads (rank-2 frontal plane), a verdict
robust to delineator and sampling rate; the total ST-threshold-event error rate's point estimates
range $\TotWrongLo$--$\TotWrongHi\%$ across the evaluated reconstructors (dominated by missed
crossings) while the false-positive/negative split is a reconstructor choice. Cross-cohort
(PTB-XL$\to$Chapman) the QRS dipolar subspace is nearly shared while ST is not---an empirical
sensitivity, not a certified transfer guarantee. We document in full a claim we abandoned---that a
diffusion reconstructor demonstrates ``fabrication is a property of the objective''---which a
leakage-corrected, multi-seed re-analysis dissolved; an exploratory null-space energy descriptor and
a greedy lead-selection heuristic are reported as such. Every number is regenerated from
lineage-stamped artifacts.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}
Wearable and reduced-lead ECG acquisition makes lead \emph{reconstruction} ubiquitous: recover
the standard 12 leads from one, three, or six observed leads
\cite{gradowski2025reveals,cardoso2024bayesian}. A low aggregate error certifies nothing about
\emph{which} morphological features are trustworthy: a cardiologist reads the P wave, the QRS
complex, the ST segment and the T wave, each on specific leads, and a reconstruction that gets
the mean right but invents an ST deviation invents a finding.

We ask a sharper, reconstructor-independent question: \emph{for a given observed lead set,
which target lead's low-rank morphology is even identifiable, and how well-conditioned is its
recovery?} Each waveform segment's instantaneous potential is approximately low rank across
leads; we estimate a per-segment rank-3 spatial basis $\bm M_s$ (top-3 singular vectors of the
population lead covariance). We stress that $\bm M_s$ is a \emph{population-estimated (PCA)}
object: on real PTB-XL its three principal angles to the classical vectorcardiographic
(inverse-Dower) space are graded---one close ($\PhysMinAngLo$--$\PhysMinAngHi^\circ$), one
moderate ($\PhysMidAngLo$--$\PhysMidAngHi^\circ$), and one substantially different
($\PhysMaxAngLo$--$\PhysMaxAngHi^\circ$)---so we call it an empirical rank-3 subspace rather than
a physical dipole (Sec.~\ref{sec:exp}).

\textbf{Contributions.} Beyond the per-lead certificate (Sec.~\ref{sec:cert}, proof
App.~\ref{app:proof}), this extended version of a four-page conference submission adds: (1) an exact
per-lead \emph{minimax ambiguity floor}---the exact minimax risk of the target's dipolar functional
over a second-moment class---which, on real data, \emph{predicts} the measured per-lead dipolar error
of the evaluated reconstructors (Prop.~\ref{thm:floor}, Fig.~\ref{fig:certval}); (2)
delineator/sampling-rate robustness of the exact ST verdict (Sec.~\ref{sec:limits}); (3) an empirical
cross-cohort sensitivity study (PTB-XL$\to$Chapman) with a $\sin\Theta$ diagnostic of
dipolar-subspace agreement (Sec.~\ref{sec:cross}); and (4) a complete account of a claim we abandoned
during pre-submission review, together with two \emph{exploratory} descriptors---a null-space energy
ratio and a greedy lead-selection heuristic---reported as exploratory, not certified
(Sec.~\ref{sec:generative}, Sec.~\ref{sec:exp}). We report the abandonment in detail on purpose:
hiding the earlier, more exciting numbers would be precisely the selective reporting our certificate
is designed to expose.

\section{Related Work}
\label{sec:related}
\textbf{Lead reconstruction and VCG synthesis.} Fixed linear transforms synthesize the
vectorcardiogram or missing leads from a standard set (inverse-Dower \cite{edenbrandt1988dower},
Kors regression \cite{kors1990vcg}), and reduced-lead reconstruction has a long clinical
literature \cite{nelwan2004reduced}; recent deep and arbitrary-mask waveform-completion models
push reconstruction accuracy \cite{gradowski2025reveals,cardoso2024bayesian,lence2025ecgrecover,%
fang2026imputeecg}. These target the reconstruction; we target the \emph{prior} question of what
a given configuration can support, independent of the reconstructor. Accordingly our neural
baseline is a \emph{representative} masked-completion U-Net of this family, included to show the
certificate's verdict still \emph{predicts} a flexible learned reconstructor's error---not a claim to beat
\cite{lence2025ecgrecover,fang2026imputeecg}. \textbf{Identifiability and lead selection.} Whether a linear functional is
recoverable from a sub-observation is the classical estimability question
\cite{scheffe1959anova}; choosing which leads to observe is optimal sensor selection
\cite{joshi2009sensor}. Our per-lead $\eta$ is estimability specialized to the ECG dipolar
functional; the novelty is not the identity but the graded, empirically-estimated,
per-target-lead map with calibrated per-record error attached. \textbf{Uncertainty for inverse
problems.} Conformal prediction \cite{romano2019cqr,angelopoulos2023gentle} gives
distribution-free intervals under exchangeability; we apply Mondrian CQR per $(S,s,\ell)$ group.

\section{Experiments}
\label{sec:exp}
\textbf{Data.} PTB-XL \cite{wagner2020ptbxl} (21{,}799 twelve-lead records, 100/500\,Hz,
10-fold split) with P/QRS/ST/T delineation by NeuroKit2 \cite{makowski2021neurokit}; the map is a
deterministic function of the estimated $\bm\mu_s,\bm M_s$ (record-level bootstrap CIs), not a
held-out evaluation. All table numbers regenerate from result JSON via
\texttt{paper/emit\_baseline\_table.py}.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../results/certificate_validation.png}
  \caption{\textbf{The certificate is operational.} Across $\CertValNcells$ (config, segment,
  target-lead) cells on held-out fold-10 records, the theoretical per-lead expected ambiguity
  $a_{s,\ell}(S)$ predicts the \emph{measured} per-lead dipolar-projection error of the dipolar,
  ridge, and OLS reconstructors. (a) $a_{s,\ell}(S)$ strongly predicts and ranks the measured error
  (Spearman $\CertValSpearAmb$, Pearson $\CertValPearAmb$). The minimax floor $a_{s,\ell}(S)$
  (Prop.~\ref{thm:floor}, $y{=}x$) is a \emph{worst-case-prior} lower bound, not an empirical envelope:
  on the true
  (non-Gaussian) prior, estimators that exploit predictable population structure beat it on a fraction
  of the $\CertValNetaPos$ non-trivial ($\eta{>}0$) cells (the pure-dipolar reconstructor on
  $\CertValViolDip$, ridge/OLS on $\CertValViolRidge$)---exactly the Tier-II residual the calibrated
  intervals of Sec.~\ref{sec:calib} meter, with the below-floor gap measuring how much structure each
  exploits. (b) The binary verdict separates recoverability: median dipolar error $\CertValMedIdent$\,mV
  for identifiable ($\eta{=}0$) vs $\CertValMedUnident$\,mV for unidentifiable ($\eta{>}0$) leads (AUC
  $\CertValAUC$). Regenerated from \texttt{results/certificate\_validation.json}.}
  \label{fig:certval}
\end{figure}

\textbf{The certificate predicts measured error.} On held-out records the theoretical expected
ambiguity $a_{s,\ell}$ and the measured per-lead dipolar-projection RMSE are strongly rank- and
value-correlated (Spearman $\CertValSpearAmb$, Pearson $\CertValPearAmb$; Fig.~\ref{fig:certval}a),
the identifiability verdict $\eta{>}0$ predicts a large measured error with AUC $\CertValAUC$, and
$\kappa$ predicts the residual error \emph{within} the identifiable regime---so the map is not a
formal artifact but an operational predictor of what each reconstructor can and cannot recover.
\input{results_v2_maps}
\input{results_v2_baseline}

\paragraph{An exploratory prescriptive use.} Because $a_{s,\ell}(S)$ is a function of the chosen
leads, one can also \emph{select} them: minimising the total ambiguity
$J(S)=\sum_{s,\ell}a_{s,\ell}(S)^2$ over an \emph{abstract lead-channel budget} $k$, choosing among
the eight independent measured lead channels (we do \emph{not} model physical electrode placement or
Wilson-terminal constraints, so this is a channel-selection abstraction, not an electrode-placement
recommendation). As an \emph{exploratory} result on this single fitted cohort, greedy forward
selection matches the exhaustive optimum at every budget, and the ambiguity reduction satisfies the
diminishing-returns inequality in $\ActiveSubmod$ of $200$ randomized tests---an empirical check, not
a submodularity proof, so we claim \emph{no} approximation guarantee. Descriptively the most
informative single channel is $\ActiveKoneLead$, the best pair $\{\ActiveKtwoSet\}$
($J{=}\ActiveKtwoJ$ vs $\ActiveKtwoRand$\,mV$^2$ for a random pair), and three chosen channels
$\{\ActiveKthreeSet\}$ suffice to reach $J{=}0$ (they span the rank-3 dipole). Cross-cohort and
bootstrap stability of the selection would be needed before any placement recommendation
(\texttt{experiments/active\_selection.py}).

\input{results_long_crossdata}

\input{results_long_generative}

\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limits}
\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../results/ablations.png}
  \caption{Sensitivities of the certificate. (a) Rank sweep: cumulative explained variance of the
  per-segment dipolar subspace; the \emph{a-priori} rank-3 choice (the cardiac dipole is 3-D)
  captures the bulk (QRS/ST). (b) $\varrho$-stability: global conditioning $\kappa_s(S)$ is flat in
  the truncation tolerance for the deployed observed-lead sets (near-rank-deficient sets, reported
  in \texttt{results/recoverability\_maps.json}, are $\varrho$-sensitive). (c) Estimated-subspace
  sensitivity: max principal angle per segment under a NORM$\to$MI diagnosis shift and under the
  12-vs-8-lead weighting; the low-amplitude ST/T subspaces move most (near/above $45^\circ$ for the
  weighting), which is why we make no fine ST/T ordering claim. (d) Rank-3 contingency of the exact
  limb-6 verdict: at retained rank~2 the limb-6 observation is itself rank-2, so all precordial
  $\tilde\eta{=}0$ (a spurious ``identifiable'' collapse); the precordial non-identifiability appears
  only at rank~$\ge$3, so the exact verdict is exact \emph{given} the a-priori rank-3 dipole model.}
  \label{fig:ablation}
\end{figure}
The subspace $\bm M_s$ is estimated, so $\eta/\kappa$ inherit its estimation error (reported via
record-bootstrap CIs and the rank/$\varrho$/subspace-angle sensitivities of
Fig.~\ref{fig:ablation}) and its cohort dependence (Sec.~\ref{sec:cross}); the ST/T third
direction is cohort-specific. Since $\eta$ depends on $\bm M_s$ only through its column space, cross-cohort
drift is governed by the principal angle between the cohorts' dipolar subspaces (Sec.~\ref{sec:transfer});
empirically this angle is small for QRS ($\TransferAngleQRS^\circ$, measured drift $\TransferDriftQRS$)
and near-orthogonal for ST ($\TransferAngleST^\circ$, uncertain drift)---an empirical two-cohort
sensitivity, not a certified transfer guarantee (the $\sin\Theta$ inequality is vacuous at the observed
conditioning). Identifiability is stated for the low-rank (dipolar) coordinate:
a reconstructor may still recover predictable non-dipolar structure (Sec.~\ref{sec:exp}), so
$\eta{>}0$ bounds the dipolar component, not every waveform detail. Conformal coverage is
within-$(S,s,\ell)$-group marginal under exchangeability, not conditional on diagnosis; rare
diagnostic subgroups are under-covered (Sec.~\ref{sec:calib}). Delineation uses NeuroKit2 and
inherits its errors, but the ST-safety endpoint is robust to it: across a delineator$\times$rate
grid (\texttt{dwt}/\texttt{cwt} $\times$ 100/500\,Hz) the precordial-not-recoverable verdict is
sign-stable ($\eta{>}0$ at every setting, as forced by the structural rank-2 limb subspace), the
per-lead recoverability ordering is preserved (Spearman $\ge\RobOrderRhoMin$), the rate axis is
tight (strong-lead $|\Delta\eta|\le\RobRateDetaStrong$), and the endpoint stays FN-dominated
(under-warning, the conservative direction) in every setting
(\texttt{results/delineator\_robustness.json}). The certificate is a pre-reconstruction screen, not
a substitute for clinical validation.

\section{Conclusion}
Reduced-lead ECG reconstruction has a known low-rank structure per segment. Exploiting it gives
a graded, per-target-lead recoverability map---what is identifiable, how well-conditioned, and
(via calibration) what interval the predictable residual admits---before and independent of any
reconstructor, plus a total ST-threshold error rate whose point estimates range
$\TotWrongLo$--$\TotWrongHi\%$ across the evaluated reconstructors. It converts ``trust the
reconstruction'' into a per-feature, per-lead, calibrated statement, and it told us honestly
when one of our own earlier claims was not supported.

\appendix
\section{Proof of Proposition~\ref{thm:perlead}}
\label{app:proof}
We work with the \emph{exact} $\bm M_{s,S}=\bm{\mathrm{Sel}}_S\bm M_s$, its exact Moore--Penrose
inverse $\bm M_{s,S}^{+}$, and the exact orthogonal row-space projector
$\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}}=\bm M_{s,S}^{+}\bm M_{s,S}$ (projecting onto $\operatorname{row}(\bm
M_{s,S})=\ker(\bm M_{s,S})^{\perp}$).

\textbf{(i) Identifiability.} The dipolar part of lead $\ell$ is the linear functional
$f_\ell(\bm d)=\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s\bm d$. From $S$ we observe $\bm M_{s,S}\bm d$, which fixes
$\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}}\bm d$ exactly; the exact kernel component $(\bm I-\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}})\bm
d$ is unobserved. Decompose $\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s=\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s\bm
P_{\mathrm{obs}}+\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s(\bm I-\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}})$. If
$\eta_{s,\ell}(S)=\|\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s(\bm I-\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}})\|_2=0$ then $f_\ell(\bm
d)=\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}}\bm d$ depends on $\bm d$ only through the observed
$\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}}\bm d$, hence is determined by $\bm M_{s,S}\bm d$: identifiable. Conversely
if $\eta_{s,\ell}(S)>0$ there is a unit $\bm\delta\in\ker\bm M_{s,S}$ with $\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm
M_s\bm\delta\neq0$; then $\bm d$ and $\bm d+t\bm\delta$ give identical observations for all $t$
but change $f_\ell$ by $t\,\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s\bm\delta$, so no estimator recovers $f_\ell$
at any SNR. This is the exact estimability criterion \cite{scheffe1959anova} for $f_\ell$.

\textbf{Exact vs.\ $\varrho$-truncated verdict (numerical check).} The deployed heatmap replaces
$\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}}$ by the rank-$r$ truncation $\bm P_{\mathrm{obs}}^{(\varrho)}=\bm V_r\bm
V_r^{\top}$ (singular directions with $\sigma_i\le\varrho\,\sigma_{\max}$ dropped). This can
disagree with the exact verdict only near a truncation boundary, and only in the
\emph{conservative} direction: $\eta^{(\varrho)}_{s,\ell}\ge0$ can be positive where
$\eta_{s,\ell}=0$ (a tiny-but-nonzero singular value is exactly identifiable yet not stably
recoverable at resolution $\varrho$), but not vice versa. We verify all three regimes in code
(\texttt{tests/test\_exact\_vs\_rho.py}): (a) an exactly rank-deficient $\bm M_{s,S}$ gives
$\eta=\eta^{(\varrho)}>0$; (b) $\bm M_{s,S}=\operatorname{diag}(1,1,10^{-6})$ gives $\eta=0$ but
$\eta^{(\varrho)}>0$ at $\varrho=10^{-2}$; (c) a full-rank well-conditioned $\bm M_{s,S}$ gives
$\eta=\eta^{(\varrho)}=0$. The limb-6 flagship falls in regime (a) exactly (rank~2), so its
verdict is $\varrho$-independent.

\textbf{(ii) Conditioning.} The mean-centred estimate is
$\widehat{\bm L}_s-\bm\mu_s=\bm M_s\bm M_{s,S}^{+}(\bm y_S-\bm\mu_{s,S})$ with
$\bm y_S-\bm\mu_{s,S}=\bm M_{s,S}\bm d+\bm{\mathrm{Sel}}_S\bm r_s+\bm n$. On the identifiable
part its lead-$\ell$ error from the observed non-dipolar residual and noise is
$\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s\bm M_{s,S}^{+}(\bm{\mathrm{Sel}}_S\bm r_s+\bm n)$, and by
Cauchy--Schwarz
$|\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s\bm M_{s,S}^{+}(\bm{\mathrm{Sel}}_S\bm r_s+\bm n)|
\le\|\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm M_s\bm M_{s,S}^{+}\|_2(\|\bm{\mathrm{Sel}}_S\bm r_s\|_2+\|\bm n\|_2)
=\kappa_{s,\ell}(S)(\|\bm{\mathrm{Sel}}_S\bm r_s\|_2+\|\bm n\|_2)$, using Cauchy--Schwarz and the
triangle inequality. Each per-lead conditioning is a row norm of $\bm A=\bm M_s\bm M_{s,S}^{+}$,
so $\kappa_{s,\ell}(S)=\|\bm e_\ell^{\top}\bm A\|_2\le\|\bm A\|_2=\kappa_s(S)$; the global
$\kappa_s$ upper-bounds every per-lead value but is not their maximum. \hfill$\square$

\section{Reproducibility}
\label{app:repro}
Code, fixed seeds, an exact environment lock (\texttt{env.lock.txt}), and a one-command
pipeline (\texttt{experiments/run\_all.py}) are released; every table number is regenerated
from result JSON (\texttt{paper/emit\_baseline\_table.py}), and a CI workflow runs the test
suite on every push. The neural baseline is a 24$\to$12-channel 1-D U-Net (base width 64,
residual GroupNorm blocks, 60 epochs, 3 deterministic seeds, Adam $2{\times}10^{-4}$, MSE on masked leads, random-mask
training); the residual predictor is a gradient-boosted quantile regressor (pinball loss) with
strict fold discipline (folds 1--7 train, 9 calibrate, 10 test).
